subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear section headings that maintain the overarching theme of mathematical patterns.
Explicit research question stated and consistently referenced throughout.
Well-illustrated step-by-step construction of the Koch snowflake demonstrates methodological transparency.
weaknesses: Research question remains too broad, limiting depth and precise focus.
Methodology is implied rather than explicitly designed or justified.
Digressions into historical anecdotes and aesthetics dilute the narrow mathematical focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall into the 3â4 band. Topic communication and RQ clarity are adequate but lack precision; methodology covers a reasonable range but lacks rigorous justification. Placement at the mid-band (4/6) reflects clear organization and purpose with room for a more focused scope and explicit methodological design.

strengths: Demonstrates sound knowledge of elementary geometry, fractals, and sequences with correct formulae in the Koch snowflake section.
Concise, clear definitions of Euclidean geometry and tessellation that support later discussion.
Accurate use of key terminology (e.g., golden ratio, bilateral symmetry) for most of the report.
weaknesses: Occasional conflation of Fibonacci spiral with logarithmic spiral without proof or derivation.
Notation inconsistencies (e.g., âSâ vs. âs,â typos) that occasionally confuse equations.
Some explanations remain superficial and rely on non-rigorous or popular sources.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands reside solidly in the 3â4 band. The student shows good subject knowledge and mostly accurate use of terminology, but lapses in mathematical rigor and consistency prevent placement in the top band. A mid-band score of 3/6 accurately reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: Choice of examples (Escher, Alhambra, sunflower, Koch snowflake) is generally relevant and supported by an extensive bibliography.
Historical context (e.g., Alhambra tiling) is well integrated, demonstrating purposeful research.
Discussion of digital tessellation and GPU rendering shows innovative application beyond classical art.
weaknesses: Analysis is largely descriptive, with most conclusions unsupported by quantitative or critical reasoning.
Minimal critical evaluation of sources or exploration of limitations and alternative viewpoints.
Heavy reliance on popular or educational websites rather than scholarly texts limits research depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands of research quality (4), analysis depth (2) and evaluation (2) locate the work in the 4â6 band but at its lower boundary. Overall critical thinking is limited by superficial analysis and minimal critical evaluation, justifying a score of 4/12.

strengths: Report follows a clear EE structure: title page, table of contents, numbered sections, and bibliography.
Effective use of headings and labelled figures enhances navigability and reader comprehension.
Word count and title are correctly placed at the start, demonstrating attention to formal requirements.
weaknesses: Page numbering is inconsistent and some figure captions are incomplete.
Formatting conventions (italics, citation style) are only partially observed.
Section titles sometimes repeat and page references in the contents page are misaligned.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure merits placement in the 3â4 band, while layout and formatting fall into the 1â2 band. A combined score of 3/4 reflects an overall clear organization with recurring minor formatting lapses.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The research questionââHow do visual representations in nature and artwork provide insights into mathematical concepts?ââis explicitly stated and consistently referenced in the introduction and conclusion.  It is understandable and linked to the ensuing discussion.

Yet the question is very wide for a 4 000-word EE; it encourages a survey rather than a focused mathematical investigation.  Because this breadth limits depth and tight connection between evidence and argument, clarity is present but focus only partial, justifying a 3 within the 3-4 band.

The analysis seldom extends beyond description.  Only the Koch snowflake section ventures into multi-step derivation; elsewhere the student reports facts (e.g., âhoneycomb optimises waxâ) without quantitative justification.  Conclusions about optimisation or beauty are asserted rather than demonstrated.

Because some relevant analysis exists but support is thin and often anecdotal, performance aligns with the 1-3 band, mid-point 2.

Key terms such as tessellation, bilateral symmetry, Euclidean geometry, golden ratio and fractal are introduced and generally used correctly.  Mathematical notation for the Koch series and basic spiral equations is mostly accurate.

However, there are occasional lapses (e.g., â\frac{s}{q}â typo, inconsistent capitalisation, âDecisionâ instead of âDivisionâ of the Plane).  Because terminology is mostly, but not consistently, precise, a 3 in the 3-4 band is appropriate.

The student includes word count, title, and numerous labelled figures.  Formatting conventions (italics for titles, superscript citations) are partially observed.  Page numbers appear in the table of contents but are not evident in the extract, and some figure captions are incomplete.

Given these minor but recurring lapses, layout is partially correct rather than fully correct, placing it in the 1-2 band with 2 marks.

The student demonstrates sound knowledge of elementary geometry, sequences, and fractals and applies these ideas to Escher prints, honeycombs, and sunflowers.  The Koch-snowflake section contains correct formulae and shows an attempt at infinite-series reasoning.

Nevertheless, several explanations stay superficial, rely on non-rigorous references, and sometimes blur mathematical accuracy (e.g., conflating Fibonacci spiral with logarithmic spiral without proof).  The work therefore shows goodâbut not excellentâsubject knowledge, fitting mid-band.

The student states the overarching themeâmathematical ideas in nature and artâand reiterates it through clear section headings (tessellations, fractals, golden ratio, etc.).  This creates an identifiable line of inquiry and indicates an intended purpose: to illustrate how visual patterns reveal underlying mathematics.  

However, the explanation remains largely descriptive and quite broad; there is little narrowing of scope to a specific mathematical problem or hypothesis.  Because focus is only partially maintained and digressions into historical anecdotes or general aesthetics dilute precision, the work fits the middle band, meriting the lower end of 3-4.

The report follows a clear EE structure with title page, contents, sequentially numbered sections and a final bibliography.  Headings guide the reader logically from tessellations to the golden ratio and a closing conclusion, aiding comprehension.

Some sections are uneven in length and there is occasional redundancy, but overall the structure is appropriate and supports understanding, meriting the 3-4 band at 3.

The bibliography is extensive and examples chosen (Escher, Alhambra, sunflower, Koch snowflake) are generally relevant to the question, demonstrating purposeful research.  Cross-referenced images and citations show that most material directly supports the topic.

Quality is limited by heavy dependence on general-interest or educational websites rather than scholarly texts, but relevance remains high, justifying placement in the 4-6 band at the lower mark of 4.

Argumentation is largely narrative: a succession of examples linked by the claim that mathematics is everywhere.  Evaluation of sources, limitations, or competing viewpoints is minimal; the brief comment on subjectivity of beauty is the only evaluative note.

Consequently, discussion remains basic and only superficially critical, corresponding to the lower 1-3 band with a score of 2.

Methodology is implied rather than explicitly designed: the student gathers secondary sources, adds a handful of own diagrams, and works through a single worked example (area of the Koch snowflake).  Sources are numerous but largely popular websites (Twinkl, BYJUâS, blogs), showing some but limited informed selection.

Because a reasonable range of examples is covered and at least one calculation is pursued, the methodology is more than minimal, yet the absence of primary data, rigorous selection criteria, or justification for chosen examples prevents placement in the top band.

annotations.comment: Definition of Euclidean geometry is concise and supports later discussions on perspective.
Effective inclusion of the report title and clearly stated research question establishes context and meets formatting expectations.
Research question is broad and invites a survey rather than a focused mathematical investigation; consider narrowing scope to a specific concept or case study.
General statements on contemporary art lack specific examples; reference a case study or artist using tessellation in murals to deepen discussion.
Historical context on the Alhambra is well integrated, showcasing cultural influence on mathematical tiling.
Primary sources are limited to popular articles; incorporate scholarly texts on Islamic geometric design to enhance research quality.
Contents page presents a clear structure, but some section titles repeat and page references appear inconsistent; verify alignment between headings and actual page numbers.
The table of contents enhances navigability, supporting reader understanding and reflecting thoughtful structure.
The introductory definition relies heavily on generalist sources and offers a descriptive overview without critical engagement; integrate scholarly perspectives.
Citation formatting is inconsistent, with HTML artifacts (<sup> tags) visible; apply consistent superscript formatting for footnotes.
Discussion of Escherâs philosophical context is informative, but connection to the research question is weak; explicitly link his patterns to mathematical insight.
Footnote entries display âlt;sup>â artifacts and inconsistent numbering; revise formatting to adhere to IB citation guidelines.
Figures 1 and 2 are well chosen to illustrate Escherâs tessellations, enhancing visual engagement.
The description of Escherâs tessellation lacks quantitative analysis (e.g., symmetry group classification); include mathematical definitions of the tiling patterns.
The definition of tessellation is clear and accurate, demonstrating sound use of terminology.
Discussion of honeycomb optimization is descriptive; include a calculation showing why hexagons minimize perimeter for given area.
Analysis of pineapple tessellation highlights deformation on curved surfaces, but lacks a model for projecting hexagons onto a cylinder or sphere.
The narrative on digital tessellation processes and GPU rendering shows innovative application beyond traditional art.
Conflation of Fibonacci spiral with logarithmic spiral lacks proof; clarify differences and provide derivation for each curve.
The step-by-step construction of the Koch snowflake is clearly illustrated; author-drawn figures enhance methodological transparency.
Notation inconsistency between S and s in equations leads to confusion; standardize variable names throughout derivations.
Algebraic manipulation in simplifying the infinite sum contains a misplaced exponent and lacks intermediary explanation; include a clear step-by-step justification.
